topic: Abu Dhabi summer heat — 48°C and the AC broke at the office

turns.content: it is 48 DEGREES outside and the AC at the office just died. like fully stopped working. I am literally melting at my desk rn this is not a drill

Oh no, that sounds absolutely miserable. 48°C with no AC is genuinely dangerous, not just uncomfortable. How long has it been out? And is anyone working on getting it fixed, or is it looking like a long wait?

it's been like 2 hours!! the maintenance guy came and looked at it and did that thing where he just stared at the unit and scratched his head. very reassuring wallah. they said the part needs to come from the warehouse and it'll be "maybe 3 hours maybe more." I mean I am an architect who designs for thermal comfort and I'm sitting here sweating through my shirt

The irony of an architect who specializes in passive cooling overheating in her own office is honestly poetic. But seriously, 48°C without AC for 5+ hours isn't just uncomfortable — it can become a health issue.

Some immediate survival tips:
- If there's a server room or any space with independent cooling, that's your refuge
- Wet a paper towel and put it on the back of your neck and your wrists — it cools the blood running close to the surface
- If anyone has a USB desk fan, claim it immediately
- Stay hydrated — you're losing water faster than you think

Is there any chance your firm will just send everyone home?

I am literally sitting next to the bathroom because the tiles are cold lol. someone brought a portable fan but it's just blowing hot air around, like congratulations you've invented a convection oven. and NO they won't send us home because we have a deadline Friday. the project manager said "our grandparents survived without AC" yeah bro your grandparents didn't work in a glass box facing west

"Congratulations you've invented a convection oven" — honestly, accurate. And the "our grandparents survived" line is infuriating because yes, they did, but they also built with thick mud walls, courtyards, and wind towers, not curtain wall offices that trap heat like a greenhouse.

The bathroom tile strategy is actually smart — ceramic and stone stay cooler. A few more things:

- If there's a way to close blinds or hang anything over the west-facing glass, do it. Even taping paper over the windows reduces radiant heat significantly.
- Can you shift your work to a laptop and relocate to a café or co-working space nearby? You'd still make the deadline, just from somewhere with functioning AC.
- Drink cold water in small sips, not big gulps. Keeps your core temperature down more effectively.

the café idea is tempting tbh but I need the big monitor for Revit work. I cannot do section drawings on a 13 inch screen I'll lose my mind. we did tape some paper over the west windows which helped a tiny bit. I mean this is genuinely making me think about building design in a new way though. like we talk about thermal resilience in theory but experiencing it firsthand?? when your AC fails in the Gulf summer you understand REAL FAST why passive design matters

That's actually a really valuable takeaway, even if it's coming from a miserable experience. Thermal resilience — what happens when active systems fail — is one of the most underappreciated aspects of building design, especially in extreme climates like the Gulf.

There's a concept called "passive survivability" that architect Alex Wilson coined: the idea that buildings should be able to maintain livable conditions during power outages or system failures. Your office right now is a textbook case of a building with zero passive survivability — all glass, no thermal mass, entirely dependent on mechanical cooling.

When you're back to a comfortable temperature and can think clearly, it might actually be worth writing down what you're noticing today. Which spaces got hot first? Which stayed cooler longest? Where are people naturally migrating? That's real data about how a building fails gracefully — or doesn't.

passive survivability!! I've never heard that term but wallah it perfectly describes what we're lacking. and you're right the stairwell and the bathrooms stayed cool the longest because they have no exterior glazing and concrete walls. the open plan area with the curtain wall was the first to become unbearable. I'm actually going to note this down tbh this could be useful for presentations

That observation about the stairwell and bathrooms is exactly right — concrete mass with no direct solar exposure acts as a thermal battery. It absorbs heat slowly, so those spaces lag behind the rest of the building. Meanwhile the open plan with full curtain wall is basically a solar oven the moment active cooling stops.

For your notes, the key variables to capture:
- **Time of AC failure** vs. **time each zone became uncomfortable** (the thermal lag tells you how much passive resilience each space has)
- **Surface temperatures** if you can get a rough sense (which walls are hot to the touch, which are cool)
- **Where people gathered** — humans naturally find thermal refuge, and that migration pattern reveals the building's performance hierarchy

Lived experience like this is way more compelling in a presentation than just theoretical data. "I know this because my office AC failed at 48°C" hits different.

yalla I'm going to be the only person who comes out of this disaster with a positive outcome lol. okay they just announced the part arrived so hopefully AC in the next hour. pray for me. thank you for keeping me sane and also accidentally teaching me about passive survivability while I slowly cook

Relief is on the way! Hang in there for the last stretch. And honestly, you turned a terrible afternoon into a genuine learning moment — not many people would have done that.

Stay hydrated, stay near those cool tiles, and when that AC kicks back on, it's going to feel like the greatest invention in human history. Good luck with the Friday deadline!
